nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on outer reef edges and coral-rich areas of the Great Barrier Reef and surrounding Coral Sea, including Lord Howe Island. Tends to occur at depths of about 2–40 m (most commonly 8–25 m), where small to large midwater aggregations feed on zooplankton above coralliferous reef structure.

diet.notes: Planktivorous in the wild, feeding on zooplankton in midwater above the reef. In captivity tends to accept a varied diet of meaty frozen foods (mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, calanus, cyclops, finely chopped seafood) alongside marine flake, micro-pellets, and herbivore preparations. Multiple small feedings per day are generally advised to mirror natural grazing on plankton. Vitamin- and HUFA-enriched foods have been reported to help maintain colouration.

avoidWith: other Pomacentrid damselfish in small tanks

summary: A peaceful schooling damselfish from Australia's Great Barrier Reef, recognisable by its yellow upper body and silver flanks split by a dark diagonal stripe. Generally considered reef-safe and well-suited to small groups in mature reef aquariums.
careNotes:
The Barrier Reef Chromis (Chromis nitida) is endemic to the Western Pacific, with its range restricted to eastern Australia (Great Barrier Reef, Coral Sea) and Lord Howe Island. In the wild it tends to occur in small to large midwater aggregations above outer reef edges at roughly 2–40 m depth (most commonly 8–25 m).

Care level disagreement: LiveAquaria's broader chromis category rates the group 'easy', while LiveAquaria's species page, Fishkeeper, and Aquatics Unlimited rate Chromis nitida specifically as 'moderate'. The averaged rating used here is 'intermediate' — the species itself is hardy, but it has been reported to be unusually susceptible to the ciliate parasite Uronema marinum, and newly imported specimens are commonly reported to benefit from an extended quarantine.

Adult size: FishBase records a maximum of 8.9 cm SL; LiveAquaria and Aquatics Unlimited cite ~4 in (≈10.2 cm); Fishkeeper records 8.5 cm. A range of 8–10 cm reflects these figures.

Minimum tank size: LiveAquaria and Aquatics Unlimited list 30 US gallons (≈114 L) for a single specimen, while Fishkeeper recommends a system of at least 120 × 45 × 45 cm (≈240 L) for a group of six or more. Because this species has been documented as shoaling, with conspecific dominance hierarchies that can stress subordinates in small volumes, the cautious-larger figure of ~200 L (with a 120 cm minimum footprint for a small group) is used here.

Water temperature: LiveAquaria/Aquatics Unlimited give 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase reports a preferred range of 22.9–27.7°C (mean 25.9°C); Fishkeeper notes 23–27°C. Averaged to approximately 23–27°C.

Colouration: A dark diagonal stripe runs from behind the eye to the rear of the dorsal fin; the upper body is yellow and the lower body silver. The common names 'Yellowback Puller' and 'Shining Puller' refer to this contrasting pattern.

Conspecific dynamics: Like other Chromis species, hierarchies have been documented in captive groups, with larger individuals harassing subordinates in undersized tanks. Keeping six or more in a sufficiently large reef tank has been reported to disperse aggression more evenly. Single specimens have been used successfully as 'dither fish' for shy tankmates.

Reproduction: Oviparous with distinct pair bonding. Demersal adhesive eggs are deposited on hard substrate and guarded/aerated by the male. Captive spawning has been reported only rarely, and rearing of pelagic larvae is considered difficult.

Disease note: Aquatics Unlimited highlights this species' susceptibility to Uronema marinum and uses a 28-day quarantine protocol. A quarantine period and vitamin-enriched diet are generally advised.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pair bonding. Eggs are demersal and adhere to hard substrate; males guard and aerate the clutch. Spawning in home aquaria has been reported but is uncommon, and captive rearing of the small pelagic larvae is considered difficult.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference: minimum tank 30 gal, temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, max ~4 in, reef compatible yes, care moderate, diet omnivore, lifespan 8–15 yrs.

additional.name: FishBase

IUCN Least Concern.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; pair bonding documented. Demersal adhesive eggs guarded and aerated by the male. Captive spawning is uncommon and rearing the small pelagic larvae is considered difficult.
